concepts.title: The Mauryan Empire was a major center of Buddhism
concepts.description: The Mauryan Empire under Ashoka played a crucial role in the spread of Buddhism throughout India and beyond, with Ashoka himself converting to Buddhism after the Kalinga War.

concepts.title: The Stupa was a symbol of Buddhist worship
concepts.description: The Stupa was a sacred structure in Buddhism, representing the Buddha's relics and serving as a focal point for Buddhist worship and meditation.

concepts.title: The concept of Ahimsa in Jainism
concepts.description: Ahimsa, or non-violence, is a fundamental principle in Jainism, emphasizing the avoidance of harm to all living beings and the pursuit of a life of non-violence and compassion.

concepts.title: The 4 main components of a Stupa
concepts.description: To remember the 4 main components of a Stupa - HALL, HEMIS, HATI, and HARMYA - use the sentence 'Hens Hide Hats In Hams' and associate each word with a component.

concepts.title: Confusing Hinduism and Jainism
concepts.description: Students often confuse Hinduism and Jainism, but key differences include the emphasis on Ahimsa in Jainism, the concept of Karma in Hinduism, and the role of the gods in each religion.

concepts.title: The 3 main periods of Indian History
concepts.description: The 3 main periods of Indian History - the Indus Valley Civilization, the Vedic Period, and the Mauryan Empire - are critical to understanding the development of Indian society and culture.

concepts.title: The significance of the Buddhist concept of Dhamma
concepts.description: Dhamma, or the universal law, is a central concept in Buddhism, emphasizing the importance of living in harmony with the natural order of the universe and following the Eightfold Path.

concepts.title: The concept of Karma in Hinduism
concepts.description: Karma, or the law of cause and effect, is a fundamental principle in Hinduism, emphasizing the idea that an individual's actions in this life will determine their circumstances in the next life.
